sections.text: Welcome to AP Biology — Module Twelve, Ecology: Populations. This kicks off College Board Unit Eight, which is ten to fifteen percent of your AP exam. Today we zoom out from single organisms to whole populations: how we measure them, how they grow, what stops them from growing forever, and the life strategies species use to survive.

Picture a few insects landing on a fresh, empty patch of habitat — tons of food, no competition. For a little while they multiply explosively, the population shooting straight up. But it never lasts. Something always bends that curve back down. So hold onto this question: why can no population grow forever — and what, exactly, slams on the brakes?

Three things today. One — how we MEASURE a population: its size, its density, how individuals are spread out, and the births, deaths, and migration that drive change. Two — how we MODEL growth, with the exponential and logistic curves and what carrying capacity really means. Three — what LIMITS populations and the survival strategies species evolve. Energy flow gets just one preview sentence — its full treatment waits for Module Fourteen.

Start with two numbers. Population size, written as N, is simply the total count of individuals. Population density is how tightly packed they are — individuals per unit area, or per unit volume. The same head count can mean very different densities depending on how much space they're spread across, so keep size and density separate in your head.

Now, HOW are individuals arranged in space? Three patterns. Clumped is by far the most common — organisms cluster where resources are patchy. Uniform is even spacing, and it usually comes from territorial behavior, as individuals push each other apart to defend space. Random spacing is rare in nature — it means no strong attraction and no strong repulsion between individuals.

What makes a population change? Demography: births and immigration ADD individuals; deaths and emigration REMOVE them. Here b, d, i, and e are per-capita rates — per individual, per unit time — so r, births minus deaths plus immigration minus emigration, is the per-capita growth rate. Positive r grows, zero holds steady, negative shrinks. The classic mistake? Forgetting immigration and emigration.

When resources are unlimited, populations grow exponentially: dN/dt equals r times N — the bigger the population, the faster it grows, sweeping into a J-curve. A shortcut: doubling time is roughly seventy divided by the percent growth rate — that r is a percentage, not the decimal r here. Pure exponential growth is rare, seen briefly during early colonization or right after a disturbance.

Resources run out, so growth follows the logistic model: dN/dt equals r times N, times K minus N over K. When N is small the bracket's near one and growth is fast; as N nears K it shrinks toward zero, flattening into an S-curve. But K isn't a hard ceiling — populations can overshoot, then oscillate or crash back toward it. K is the settling level.

Here's the trap the AP loves. Exponential growth, the J-curve, assumes unlimited resources and accelerates without end — but it's rare and short-lived. Logistic growth, the S-curve, is reined in by carrying capacity, settling toward K, though populations can overshoot and oscillate back. Read the scenario: unlimited food and freshly colonized means exponential; slowing near the environment's limit means logistic.

What knocks a population down or holds it back? The deciding test is the mechanism: does the factor's per-capita effect change with N? If it strengthens because there are MORE individuals, it's density-dependent — competition, disease, predation, waste, territoriality. If not, it's density-independent — weather extremes, fire, floods, volcanic eruptions, human disturbance. Classify by that test, not the lists; the same factor can flip by scenario.

Your turn — don't just match the example to a bin. Scenario A: a wildfire kills a larger fraction of individuals in a DENSER stand than a sparse one. Scenario B: a long cold snap kills many individuals regardless of crowding. For each, density-dependent or independent? Use the test — does the per-capita effect change with N? Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you're ready.

Let's check — notice the twist. Scenario A is density-DEPENDENT: though fire is the classic density-independent example, HERE its per-capita effect rises with crowding — the denser stand burns faster — so it intensifies with N. Scenario B is density-INDEPENDENT: a cold snap strikes regardless of crowding. So classify by the mechanism, does the per-capita effect change with N, not the example label.

Species also differ in their whole life strategy. r-selected species bet on quantity: many offspring, little or no parental care, short lives, and they reproduce early — think insects, weeds, many fish. K-selected species bet on quality: few offspring, heavy parental care, long lives, late reproduction — humans, elephants, large mammals. But don't force every organism into one bin: most lie somewhere on the spectrum between the extremes.

A survivorship curve graphs survival across a lifetime, with age across the bottom and the number still alive going up. Type One stays high then drops late — most survive early and middle life and die old, like humans and large mammals, the K-selected pattern. Type Two is a straight diagonal: constant mortality at every age. Type Three plunges early then flattens — huge early losses, few survivors, the r-selected pattern like oysters and many fish.

One quick bridge before we wrap. Unit Eight also covers how energy moves through ecosystems, and the single idea to file away is this: matter cycles, but energy flows one way and is ultimately lost as heat — it is not recycled. That's all we need today; the full treatment of energy flow waits for Module Fourteen.

Recap. Measure by size, density, and dispersion; track change with per-capita r equals births minus deaths plus immigration minus emigration. Model growth two ways: exponential dN/dt equals rN; logistic, the S-curve, settling toward K — not an unbreakable ceiling, since populations can overshoot and oscillate back. Classify limiting factors by mechanism. Survivorship Type One maps to K, Type Three to r. Matter cycles, energy flows.

Your path forward. Practice the four moves the AP rewards: classify a limiting factor by the mechanism test — does its per-capita effect change with N; spot exponential versus logistic; match a species to survivorship type and strategy; find the sign of per-capita r. Do the GIIS Unit Eight problem set in your dashboard, and grab an advisor check-in if any of these still feel shaky. Next: Module Thirteen.
